mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Lithuania on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe — northern Europe, on the Baltic Sea. 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Lithuania?
mapQuestions.answer: Vilnius — known for its giant Baroque Old Town.

mapQuestions.question: Which countries border Lithuania?
mapQuestions.answer: Latvia to the north, Belarus to the east, Poland to the south, and Russia (Kaliningrad) to the south-west.

mapQuestions.question: Lithuania is geographically interesting — where is the centre of Europe officially located?
mapQuestions.answer: Just outside Vilnius, marked by a monument. The exact "centre of Europe" is disputed, but a French study placed it here in 1989.

mapQuestions.question: What is the Curonian Spit?
mapQuestions.answer: A 98 km long thin sandy peninsula shared with Russia, with the world's tallest drifting sand dunes outside the Sahara.

foods.name: Cepelinai
foods.description: Massive potato dumplings shaped like little blimps (named after Zeppelins), stuffed with minced meat, served with bacon and sour cream.

foods.name: Šaltibarščiai
foods.description: Hot pink cold beetroot soup with kefir, dill, cucumber, and a hard-boiled egg. Looks dramatic, tastes refreshing in summer.

foods.name: Kepta duona
foods.description: Fried rye bread sticks with garlic and cheese — Lithuanian pub snack at its finest.

foods.name: Bulvių blynai
foods.description: Crispy fried potato pancakes with sour cream or apple sauce. Comfort food.

foods.name: Šakotis
foods.description: A "tree cake" — batter dripped onto a spit and slowly rotated over an open flame, building up dripping spiky layers. Looks like a Christmas tree.

foods.name: Skilandis
foods.description: A smoked, dry-cured pig stomach stuffed with chopped meat, garlic and pepper. Sliced thin like salami, served on dark rye bread. A traditional Lithuanian smallholding food.

scavengerItems.label: A castle on an island or a hill
A bakery with at least 5 rye breads
A forest within walking distance of a town centre
A basketball hoop in a public space (basketball is the national obsession)
A wooden cross or crucifix beside a road
A book in Lithuanian — count the consonants

geoMatches.description: The capital. Largest Baroque Old Town in Eastern Europe, narrow cobbled streets, and the bohemian "Užupis" district that declared itself a "republic".
Lithuania's second city, with a fortress, modernist architecture from the 1920s-30s, and a strong cultural scene.
A medieval island castle on a lake — picture-perfect red brick fortress, accessible only by a wooden bridge.
A thin 98 km sand peninsula with giant drifting dunes — UNESCO-listed, shared with Russia.
A small hill near Šiauliai covered in more than 100,000 wooden and metal crosses, left by pilgrims over 200 years.

senses.smell: Wood smoke, rye bread, pine forest, beeswax candles in old churches…
senses.hear: Vilnius church bells (over 60 churches in the Old Town), basketball games on TV in every bar, "Labas!" greetings…
senses.taste: Heavy potato dumplings, sour cream on everything, smoked meats, cool kefir, wild honey…
senses.feel: Cobblestones underfoot, cold Baltic wind, scratchy linen tablecloths, smooth amber…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: What is the "Republic of Užupis"?
stories.body: Across a small river in central Vilnius is a neighbourhood called Užupis ("across the river"). In 1997, artists and bohemians who lived there jokingly declared it an independent republic — with its own flag, constitution, president, and a 12-strong army. Today the "Republic" celebrates its independence day on April 1st with parades, free beer, and stamps in tourist passports. The constitution is hilarious and posted on a wall in 30+ languages: "A dog has the right to be a dog", "Everyone has the right to be happy", "Everyone has the right to be unhappy", "Everyone has the right to die, but it is not a duty". It's entirely tongue-in-cheek but feels real.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If you and your friends declared your bedroom a "republic", what would be in its constitution?

stories.question: Why are there 100,000 crosses on one hill?
stories.body: The Hill of Crosses (Kryžių kalnas) is a small hill in northern Lithuania covered with crosses of every size and material — wood, metal, stone — placed by pilgrims, mourners, and the faithful for over 200 years. It started in the 1830s after a Polish-Lithuanian uprising. During the Soviet era, the Communist authorities bulldozed the crosses three times because the site's religion threatened communist ideology — and every time, within days, more crosses appeared overnight. Today there are over 100,000. Pope John Paul II visited in 1993 and called it a place of hope, peace, and love. Standing among them is otherworldly.

stories.question: How can there be giant sand dunes in northern Europe?
stories.body: The Curonian Spit is a thin 98 km strip of sand between the Baltic Sea and a lagoon, shared with Russia. The dunes here are some of the tallest drifting dunes in Europe — over 60 m high. They move with the wind, slowly burying forests and even entire villages. Four villages were swallowed by the dunes in the 1800s. To stop the dunes destroying more towns, Lithuanians planted pine forests to anchor the sand. Today the spit is UNESCO-listed for both its nature and its tiny fishing villages with wooden houses.

stories.question: What was the Baltic Way?
stories.body: On 23 August 1989, two million people from Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania held hands in a continuous human chain across all three countries — 675 km long. They were protesting against Soviet occupation and demanding independence. It was peaceful, silent for most of its length, and one of the most extraordinary mass protests in history. Imagine your country, plus two others, all joining hands across them. Two years later, all three Baltic states were independent. The Baltic Way is now in the UNESCO Memory of the World register.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Two million people holding hands silently. Why might silence be more powerful than shouting?

stories.question: Why is Lithuania so obsessed with basketball?
stories.body: Lithuania (population 2.7 million) is one of the world's best basketball nations — they've won European championships and Olympic medals. Basketball is the unofficial second religion. The country fell in love with the sport in the 1930s after early NBA stars Frank Lubin (Lithuanian-American) brought the modern game to Lithuania. Under Soviet rule, basketball became a way to assert Lithuanian identity — even Lithuanian players on the Soviet team felt Lithuanian first, Soviet second. The 1992 Lithuanian basketball team famously beat the post-Soviet "Unified Team" at the Barcelona Olympics, with players wearing tie-dye uniforms sponsored by the Grateful Dead.

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you've eaten in Lithuania so far?
Cepelinai — surprisingly good or way too heavy?
Latvia, Lithuania, Estonia — what's the difference, do you think?
Vilnius has more churches per square km than almost anywhere in Europe. Did you go in any?
What's one Lithuanian habit you'd like to bring home?
Lithuania declared independence first of the Soviet republics, in 1990. What does it take to be the first to do something brave?

wordSearch.hint: Lithuanian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: White stork
animals.description: Lithuania's national bird — there's a stork in almost every village in summer.

animals.name: European beaver
animals.description: Common in Lithuanian rivers and wetlands.

animals.name: Eurasian elk (moose)
animals.description: In Lithuanian forests, especially in the Dzūkija and Aukštaitija national parks.

animals.name: Wild boar
animals.description: Common in farmland and forest edges. Numbers controlled by hunting.

animals.name: Red deer
animals.description: In forests, especially the south. Watch at dawn near forest edges.

animals.name: Grey wolf
animals.description: About 400 wolves in Lithuanian forests. Numbers stable.

animals.name: Eurasian lynx
animals.description: Lithuania has Europe's healthiest lynx population — about 200, in deep forests.
